title: Higher-order exceptional points and enhanced quantum squeezing in a pseudo-Hermitian semiconductor optomechanical system
authors: Zi-Wei Jiang, Yan-Zi Jing, Zhen-Sen Lin, Rui Zhang, Wen-Quan Yang, Ya-Feng Jiao, Le-Man Kuang
abstract: We investigate higher-order exceptional points and quantum squeezing of exciton polaritons in a pseudo-Hermitian semiconductor optomechanical system. We show that a third-order exceptional point (EP3) can emerge from the tripartite coupling among photons, excitons, and phonons under pseudo-Hermitian conditions. A pronounced two-mode quantum squeezing of exciton polaritons is revealed, and we demonstrate that this squeezing is significantly enhanced in the vicinity of the EP3. Furthermore, we find that in the PT-symmetric phase, the squeezing dynamics produce a frequency comb of exciton polaritons, whereas the squeezing remains constant over time in the PT-symmetry broken phase and exactly at the EP3. The sudden change in quantum squeezing dynamics can be used to probe the phase transition and the EP3. Our work opens a pathway to manipulate quantum squeezing in semiconductor optomechanical platforms, offering potential advantages for quantum sensing and metrology.
summary: This paper theoretically explores how higher-order exceptional points (EP3) manifest in a pseudo-Hermitian semiconductor optomechanical system. It shows that the EP3 enhances two-mode quantum squeezing, and critically, the change in squeezing dynamics—from a frequency comb to a constant value—serves as a robust diagnostic tool to map out the system's quantum phase transitions.
main_problem: The study investigates the emergence of higher-order exceptional points (EP3) and their influence on quantum squeezing in a pseudo-Hermitian semiconductor optomechanical system.
main_result: The research demonstrates that an EP3 exists due to tripartite coupling, leading to enhanced two-mode quantum squeezing. The squeezing dynamics exhibit distinct behaviors (frequency comb vs. constant) depending on whether the system is in the PT-symmetric or PT-broken phase.
method: The analysis involves deriving the effective Hamiltonian, linearizing quantum fluctuations, and calculating time-dependent squeezing parameters using quadrature operators.
model_or_system: The system is a semiconductor optomechanical setup coupling photons, excitons, and phonons, modeled using a pseudo-Hermitian Hamiltonian. The analysis proceeds by examining the eigenvalues and the resulting quantum noise properties.
key_observables: Two-mode quantum squeezing parameters ($S_{XC}, S_{YC}$), the existence and location of Exceptional Points (EP3), and the time evolution of these parameters.
important_parameters: Coupling strengths ($G_{l,b}/\kappa_l$), detunings ($\Delta_u, \Delta_l$), and the dimensionless time ($\kappa l t$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the system operates in the strong-coupling regime and utilizes a linearization approach for quantum fluctuations.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the coalescence of eigenvalues at the EP3 as a function of coupling strength, and show the time evolution of squeezing, revealing a 'squeezing comb' in the PT-symmetric phase versus constant squeezing in the broken phase.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the model and Hamiltonian, then analyzes the conditions for pseudo-Hermiticity and the emergence of EPs. Subsequently, it quantifies quantum squeezing using quadrature operators, mapping out the distinct dynamical regimes around the EP3.
why_interesting: This work directly bridges non-Hermitian physics (EPs) with quantum optics (squeezing) in a solid-state platform (optomechanics), providing a mechanism to actively probe quantum phase transitions using measurable quantum noise signatures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the semicondoctor exciton- optomechanical system. (a) The tripartite coupling config- uration among the cavity mode c, the exciton mode x, and the mechanical mode b. (b) The tripartite coupling configu- ration among the cavity mode c, polariton modes au and al after linearization in the strong-coupling regime.

FIG. 2. Real and imaginary parts of the eigenvalues as func- tions of the dimensionless coupling strength Gl,b/κl. (a) and (b) show the real and imaginary parts of the eigenvalues, re- spectively. The solid (red) curve represents Ω0, the dash-dot (green) curve corresponds to Ω+, and the dot (blue) curve corresponds to Ω−. Here, λ = 2 and Ωm/κl = 1.

FIG. 3. Dynamic evolution of the quantum squeezing de- scribed by the squeezing parameter SXC versus the dimen- sionless time κlt for typical coupling strengths. (a) Cases of PT-symmetry broken phase (Gl,b/κl = 0.6, 0.8) and EP3 (Gl,b/κl = 1.0); (b)-(d) Cases of PT-symmetric phase for Gl,b/κl = 1.05, 1.10, and 1.15, respectively. Here we take λ = 2.

FIG. 4. The quantum squeezing described by the squeezing parameter SXC versus the dimensionless coupling strength Gl,b/κl at different dimensionless evolution times (a) κlt = 20; (b) κlt = 40; (c) κlt = 60; (d) κlt = 100. Here we take λ = 2. The red dot marks the EP3 at Gl,b/κl = 1.
